preamble: The transformation of the voiced palatal approximant [j] to the voiced alveolar fricative [z] is a documented sound change across various language families. This shift involves a strengthening of the articulation, moving from a less constricted approximant to a more constricted fricative, alongside a fronting of the place of articulation from the palate to the alveolar ridge.
phoneticEffects: Fortition (approximant to fricative), Fronting (palatal to alveolar), Alveolarization

examples.note: Meaning 'yoke'. Classic example of the PIE *y- to Greek z- shift.
The Proto-Kuki-Chin palatal approximant *j developed into a voiced alveolar fricative /z/ in many Kuki-Chin varieties, including Mara.
The Proto-Kuki-Chin palatal approximant *j developed into a voiced alveolar fricative /z/ in many Kuki-Chin varieties, including Tedim.
The Proto-Kuki-Chin palatal approximant *j developed into a voiced alveolar fricative /z/ in many Kuki-Chin varieties, including Thado.
The Proto-Kuki-Chin palatal approximant *j developed into a voiced alveolar fricative /z/ in many Kuki-Chin varieties, including Sizang.
The Proto-Kuki-Chin palatal approximant *j developed into a voiced alveolar fricative /z/ in many Kuki-Chin varieties, including Hakha Lai.

sources: Palmer, L. R. (1980). The Greek Language. Faber & Faber.
Van Bik, K. (2009). The sound system of Lai. Linguistics of the Tibeto-Burman Area, 20(2), 9–19.
